feature_request: The CRM should alert us when our relationship with a country goes cold.

included: Automated alerts for identifying 'cold' relationships based on engagement activity thresholds
Configuration options for defining 'cold' relationship criteria (e.g., no meetings logged, no emails sent, etc.)
Dashboard widget or report to view countries flagged as 'cold'
Role-based permissions for managing alert criteria to ensure sensitive data is handled appropriately
excluded: Prediction or AI-based analysis of relationship health beyond engagement activity thresholds
Alerts based on sentiment analysis or subjective assessments of relationship quality
Automated outreach or remediation workflows triggered by 'cold' alerts

assumptions.assumption: Engagement activity is the primary indicator of relationship status in the CRM system.
assumptions.rationale: Engagement activity is already tracked in the CRM and is a logical basis for evaluating relationship health.

assumptions.assumption: Users will want to define custom criteria for what constitutes a 'cold' relationship rather than relying on a standardized definition.
assumptions.rationale: Relationships with different countries or projects may vary in terms of expected engagement frequency.

assumptions.assumption: Sensitive data considerations require role-based permissions for managing alert criteria.
assumptions.rationale: The CRM contains diplomatic and confidential information, and access should be tightly controlled.

success_criteria: Users can configure engagement thresholds that define a 'cold' relationship.
The system reliably flags countries with insufficient engagement based on these thresholds.
Users can view flagged countries on a dashboard or report within the CRM.
Alerts are only manageable by users with the appropriate permission levels.

new_challenges.description: You assume that engagement activity is the primary indicator of relationship status (A1), but what if there are other important factors like qualitative feedback or external geopolitical events? This matters because reliance solely on engagement thresholds might lead to false positives or negatives.
What happens if the engagement data is incomplete or outdated? This could lead to inaccurate flagging of 'cold' relationships. The proposal does not address how data quality issues will be handled.

Role-based permissions are included (A3), but how will these permissions be audited? Sensitive data handling requires a mechanism for tracking who configures or views alert criteria to ensure accountability.

The term 'insufficient engagement' could mean different things depending on the context (e.g., frequency, type of activity, importance of counterpart). The proposal does not clearly define how this will be standardized or customized.
Who is responsible for responding to flagged 'cold' relationships? The proposal does not specify whether there will be a designated user role or team to act on this information.

content.assessment: The proposal is well-structured and addresses several key elements, but it currently relies on assumptions that need further examination. Critical gaps related to data quality, operational responsibilities, and security auditing remain unaddressed.
remaining_concerns: Clarification on qualitative factors affecting relationship status
Defined process for addressing flagged relationships
Mechanisms to ensure data quality and audit security actions

included: Automated alerts for identifying 'cold' relationships based on engagement activity thresholds
Configuration options for defining 'cold' relationship criteria (e.g., no meetings logged, no emails sent, etc.)
Dashboard widget or report to view countries flagged as 'cold'
Role-based permissions for managing alert criteria to ensure sensitive data is handled appropriately
Audit logs for tracking changes to alert criteria and views of sensitive data
Mechanism to flag and handle incomplete or outdated engagement data
excluded: Prediction or AI-based analysis of relationship health beyond engagement activity thresholds
Alerts based on sentiment analysis or subjective assessments of relationship quality
Automated outreach or remediation workflows triggered by 'cold' alerts

assumptions.assumption: Engagement activity is the primary indicator of relationship status in the CRM system.
assumptions.rationale: Engagement activity is a practical and measurable basis for evaluating relationship health, but qualitative or external factors could play a role.

assumptions.assumption: Users will want to define custom criteria for what constitutes a 'cold' relationship rather than relying on a standardized definition.
assumptions.rationale: Relationships with different countries or projects may vary in terms of expected engagement frequency.

assumptions.assumption: Sensitive data considerations require role-based permissions for managing alert criteria.
assumptions.rationale: The CRM contains diplomatic and confidential information, and access should be tightly controlled.

assumptions.assumption: Audit logs will be necessary to ensure accountability for sensitive data handling.
assumptions.rationale: Sensitive data handling requires oversight to prevent misuse or unauthorized changes.

assumptions.assumption: Data quality issues such as incomplete or outdated engagement records will need to be flagged and addressed.
assumptions.rationale: Data gaps could lead to inaccurate flagging, so mechanisms for identifying and resolving these issues are necessary.

success_criteria: Users can configure engagement thresholds that define a 'cold' relationship, including customization options for frequency and type of activity.
The system reliably flags countries with insufficient engagement based on these thresholds, with mechanisms to account for incomplete or outdated data.
Users can view flagged countries on a dashboard or report within the CRM.
Alerts are only manageable by users with the appropriate permission levels, and audit logs track all changes to alert criteria and views of sensitive data.
Designated teams or roles are identified to act on flagged 'cold' relationships, ensuring operational follow-up.

challenge_responses.response: Acknowledge that engagement activity alone may not fully capture relationship status. Incorporate flexibility for qualitative feedback or external geopolitical factors into the alert configuration options.
Include mechanisms to flag and address incomplete or outdated engagement data to improve accuracy of 'cold' relationship alerts.
Add audit logs to track changes to alert criteria and views of sensitive data for accountability and oversight purposes.
Maintain the position that customization options for defining 'cold' relationship criteria will allow users to standardize or adapt definitions as needed for their context.
Specify that designated teams or roles will be identified to act on flagged 'cold' relationships, ensuring operational follow-up.

new_challenges.description: While audit logs have been added, the proposal does not specify how long these logs will be retained or how access to these logs will be managed. This is crucial for ensuring accountability over time.

The proposal mentions designated teams or roles for operational follow-up on flagged 'cold' relationships, but it does not clarify how these teams will be notified or what tools they will use to address the flags efficiently.

content.assessment: The proposal has improved significantly and addresses key challenges from the previous round. Audit logs, data quality mechanisms, and operational follow-up roles have been added, strengthening the overall scope. However, some operational and security details remain ambiguous, requiring further clarification.
remaining_concerns: Retention and access management for audit logs
Notification and tooling for operational teams handling flagged relationships

included: Automated alerts for identifying 'cold' relationships based on engagement activity thresholds
Configuration options for defining 'cold' relationship criteria (e.g., no meetings logged, no emails sent, etc.)
Dashboard widget or report to view countries flagged as 'cold'
Role-based permissions for managing alert criteria to ensure sensitive data is handled appropriately
Audit logs for tracking changes to alert criteria and views of sensitive data, with configurable retention periods and access controls
Mechanism to flag and handle incomplete or outdated engagement data
Notification system to alert designated teams or roles about flagged 'cold' relationships
excluded: Prediction or AI-based analysis of relationship health beyond engagement activity thresholds
Alerts based on sentiment analysis or subjective assessments of relationship quality
Automated outreach or remediation workflows triggered by 'cold' alerts

assumptions.assumption: Engagement activity is the primary indicator of relationship status in the CRM system.
assumptions.rationale: Engagement activity is a practical and measurable basis for evaluating relationship health, but qualitative or external factors could play a role.

assumptions.assumption: Users will want to define custom criteria for what constitutes a 'cold' relationship rather than relying on a standardized definition.
assumptions.rationale: Relationships with different countries or projects may vary in terms of expected engagement frequency.

assumptions.assumption: Sensitive data considerations require role-based permissions for managing alert criteria.
assumptions.rationale: The CRM contains diplomatic and confidential information, and access should be tightly controlled.

assumptions.assumption: Audit logs will be necessary to ensure accountability for sensitive data handling.
assumptions.rationale: Sensitive data handling requires oversight to prevent misuse or unauthorized changes.

assumptions.assumption: Data quality issues such as incomplete or outdated engagement records will need to be flagged and addressed.
assumptions.rationale: Data gaps could lead to inaccurate flagging, so mechanisms for identifying and resolving these issues are necessary.

assumptions.assumption: Audit logs will need configurable retention periods and access controls to ensure accountability over time.
assumptions.rationale: Retention periods will ensure logs are available for long-term audits, and access controls will prevent misuse or unauthorized viewing.

assumptions.assumption: Designated teams or roles will need real-time notifications and operational tools to address flagged 'cold' relationships efficiently.
assumptions.rationale: Timely notifications and tools will ensure that flagged issues are acted upon promptly and effectively.

success_criteria: Users can configure engagement thresholds that define a 'cold' relationship, including customization options for frequency and type of activity.
The system reliably flags countries with insufficient engagement based on these thresholds, with mechanisms to account for incomplete or outdated data.
Users can view flagged countries on a dashboard or report within the CRM.

Alerts are only manageable by users with the appropriate permission levels, and audit logs track all changes to alert criteria and views of sensitive data, with retention periods and access controls configurable.
Designated teams or roles are notified in real-time about flagged 'cold' relationships and have access to operational tools to address these flags efficiently.

challenge_responses.response: Added configurable retention periods and access controls for audit logs to ensure accountability over time and prevent misuse or unauthorized access.
Added a notification system to alert designated teams or roles about flagged 'cold' relationships, and specified that operational tools will be provided to address these flags efficiently.

content.assessment: The proposal has addressed all previously identified challenges. Configurable retention periods and access controls for audit logs ensure accountability over time. The notification system and operational tools for designated teams resolve concerns about follow-up on flagged 'cold' relationships. The scope is now comprehensive and well-defined.
content.satisfaction_rationale: The proposal now includes all necessary elements to ensure functionality, security, and operational effectiveness. All key assumptions have been reviewed, and remaining gaps would require stakeholder-specific input rather than further deliberation.
